1: The eighth day (yom hashemini) — after the seven days of ordination confinement — is the day of new beginning in Israel's calendar. The eighth day recurs as a threshold-day: circumcision on the eighth day (Leviticus 12:3), the leper's purification begins on the eighth day after his cleansing week (Leviticus 14:10), the dedication of Solomon's temple runs through the eighth day (1 Kings 8:66; 2 Chronicles 7:9). The number eight in Israel's symbolic system stands for the day beyond the week — the beginning of a new cycle, the day that transcends the seven-day creation order. In early Christian interpretation, the eighth day becomes the day of resurrection and new creation, the eschatological day that breaks into time.
2: Moses instructs Aaron to offer for his own sin first — a bull calf for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering. The sequence is not optional: the high priest cannot serve as the people's mediator until his own sin is addressed. Hebrews 7:27 draws the explicit contrast: the Levitical high priest must offer daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people — Christ has no such need because he has no sin. The unblemished requirement for Aaron's offering mirrors the standard for all sacrificial animals and anticipates the sinless character of the ultimate high priest.
3: The people's sin offering (a male goat) is distinct from Aaron's (a bull calf) — the different animals reflect the different gravity of the high priest's representative position. The priest's sin offering is always a bull (the most costly animal); the people's offering varies by the type of sin and the social position of the offerer (Leviticus 4:3, 14, 22-28, 32). The democratic structure of the sacrificial system — different animals for leaders and commoners — reflects the Levitical principle that responsibility before YHWH scales with position.
4: “For today YHWH will appear to you” — the announcement that YHWH will appear (nireh) is the telos of the entire ordination sequence. Seven days of preparation and sacrifice culminate in theophany; the whole apparatus of the cult exists as the divinely appointed means of YHWH's self-disclosure to his people. The peace offerings (bull and ram) with their grain offering frame the theophany — when divine presence appears, it appears in the context of communal offering and communal peace.
5: The people assemble at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the same gathering-point as the ordination (Leviticus 8), but now for the first public sacrificial service. This inaugural assembly is the founding moment of Israel's corporate worship in the land — or rather, before the land, in the wilderness. The assembly at the tent entrance mirrors the assembly at Sinai, binding the cult to the covenant: sacrificial worship is covenant-worship, not a separate religious sphere.
6: “This is the thing that YHWH commanded you to do, that the glory of YHWH may appear to you” — the explicit connection between obedience to YHWH's sacrificial prescription and the appearance of divine glory. The Levitical cult is not magical manipulation but responsive obedience to YHWH's appointed means of presence. The appearing of glory is both the purpose and the validation of the cult: purpose (YHWH intended to manifest himself through these means) and validation (when his glory appears, the offering has been accepted).
7: “Draw near to the altar and offer your sin offering and your burnt offering” — Moses formally commissions Aaron to begin his priestly ministry. Qarav (draw near) is the standard term for approaching the altar and the holy things; the priesthood's essential function is to mediate the act of drawing near that ordinary Israelites cannot perform. The command-sequence — sin offering first, then burnt offering, then the people's offerings — models the order of approach to YHWH that will structure every subsequent day of priestly service.
8: Aaron approaches the altar and slaughters his sin offering calf — the transition from Moses' command to Aaron's independent action marks the beginning of the functioning priesthood. Until this moment, Moses was the officiant; now Aaron takes over. The slaughtering at the altar's north side (the prescribed location for sin offerings, Leviticus 4:29) by Aaron's own hand marks the high priest not merely as the one who receives and presents the sacrifice but as the one who performs the killing act that makes atonement possible.
9: Aaron's sons bring the blood to him — the high priest applies the blood to the altar's horns and pours the remainder at the base, but the transport of the blood-vessel from the slaughter-site to the altar is delegated to the sons. This division of labor between the high priest and the ordinary priests is the operating structure of the Levitical system: the high priest performs the key atoning acts, the regular priests assist in ancillary functions. The blood on the altar's horns (applied with the finger) is the most sacred act of the sin-offering ritual.
10: The fat portions, both kidneys, and the appendage of the liver from Aaron's sin offering are burned on the altar — the same three portions designated as YHWH's in all sin and peace offerings (Leviticus 3:14-16; Leviticus 4:8-10). The burning of these portions for Aaron's personal offering follows exactly the same protocol as for the people's offerings, establishing that even the high priest has no special dispensation from the standard ritual requirements. He is a worshipper before he is a mediator.
11: The flesh and the skin of Aaron's sin offering are burned outside the camp — the protocol for sin offerings whose blood is applied to the altar's horns (rather than the altar of incense inside the sanctuary). This burning outside the camp removes the sin-laden material completely from the community's presence. The sin is not merely covered but expelled — the burning outside the camp is the spatial enactment of sin's removal from YHWH's dwelling place among Israel.
12: Aaron slaughters the burnt offering for himself — the ram whose blood he dashes against all sides of the altar. The burnt offering follows the sin offering, enacting the two-phase logic of approach: first atonement (sin removed), then dedication (life offered). The complete burning of the burnt offering (nothing is eaten, nothing is reserved) expresses the totality of Aaron's self-dedication to the priestly office on its first day of operation.
13: Aaron's sons present the pieces of the burnt offering to him — again, the division of labor between high priest and sons. The pieces are cut according to the standard burnt-offering protocol (Leviticus 1:6-8): the head, fat, legs, and entrails separately, all to be burned on the altar. Even the cutting and presenting of pieces is an act of priestly service that implicates all members of the priestly order in the offering.
14: Aaron washes the entrails and the legs and burns them on the burnt offering on the altar. The washing of the entrails and legs (the organs most associated with bodily process and ground-contact) before burning ensures that nothing impure goes onto the altar. The thoroughness of the protocol — even the interior organs are washed — communicates that what is offered to YHWH must be as pure as the offering system can achieve. The care given to preparation reflects the altar's holiness.
15: Aaron now presents the people's sin offering — the goat — and performs the sin offering for the people “as the first” (i.e., following the same procedure as his own). The transition from Aaron's personal offering to the people's offering marks the formal activation of his intercessory function. The high priest is now fully constituted: he has offered for himself and now offers for the people, which is precisely the two-stage structure Hebrews identifies as the essential character of the Levitical high priesthood (Hebrews 5:3; Leviticus 7:27).
16: The burnt offering for the people is offered “according to the rule” (kamishpat) — the technical term for the prescribed procedure. The word mishpat in sacrificial contexts means the ordained regulation or norm; to offer kamishpat is to follow the divine prescription exactly. The phrase signals that Aaron's first official priestly act for the people adheres rigorously to the standard established in Lev 1. The new priest follows the established rule rather than innovating.
17: The grain offering is presented after the burnt offering — Aaron takes a handful and burns it on the altar in addition to the morning burnt offering (the tamid) that was already burning. The mention of the morning tamid (the perpetual daily burnt offering of Exodus 29:38-42) links Aaron's inaugural service to the ongoing daily worship cycle. The first high-priestly service does not replace the daily offering but supplements it — confirming that Aaron's ministry stands within the structure of the perpetual worship YHWH has prescribed.
18: Aaron slaughters the bull and the ram of the people's peace offerings (shelamim). The peace offering is the only sacrificial type shared among YHWH, the priests, and the offerers: fat and organs burned for YHWH, breast and right thigh for the priests, the remainder for the offerers. It is thus the covenant communion meal: all parties to the covenant share in the sacrificial meal. The inauguration of the priesthood culminates in the peace offering — the sign of restored communion between YHWH and Israel.
19: Aaron's sons present the fat portions of the bull and ram to Aaron — the fat from the tail, the covering of the entrails, both kidneys and their fat, and the appendage of the liver. The enumeration of the fat portions emphasizes the precision of the offering: every element that belongs to YHWH is carefully identified and set aside. Nothing that is designated as YHWH's portion can be withheld or diverted; the completeness of the offering is a moral as well as ritual requirement.
20: The fat portions are placed on the breasts — the fatty parts and the breasts are separated so that the fat can be burned on the altar while the breast is wave-offered. This choreography of the peace offering separates YHWH's portion (the fat, burned on the altar) from the officiating priest's portion (the breast, wave-offered then eaten) from the offerer's portion (the remaining meat). The spatial and temporal choreography of the peace offering enacts the covenant structure of shared participation.
21: Aaron waves the breasts and the right thighs as a wave offering before YHWH — the wave offering (tenufah) of the breast and right thigh constitutes the priests' portion in all peace offerings (Leviticus 7:30-34). The waving enacts the principle that even the priests' portion is first presented to YHWH before being returned to them as their share. The right thigh (shoq hayamin) is the elevated offering (terumah), slightly more valuable than the wave offering breast. The two priests' portions — wave-breast and elevated-thigh — become the basis for the regular priestly income from the people's offerings.
22: Aaron lifts his hands toward the people and blesses them — the priestly gesture of blessing with uplifted hands toward the congregation. The Aaronic blessing of Numbers 6:24-26 is the specific content of this blessing: “YHWH bless you and keep you; YHWH make his face shine on you and be gracious to you; YHWH lift up his countenance upon you and give you peace.” The blessing descends from YHWH through the priestly mediator to the people. When the blessing has been given, Aaron steps down from the altar — the sacrificial service is complete and he now addresses the congregation. Luke 24:50 notes that Jesus raised his hands and blessed his disciples at the ascension, performing the Aaronic blessing-gesture as the ultimate High Priest departing after his completed sacrifice.
23: Moses and Aaron enter the tent of meeting together — the only time Moses and Aaron enter together; this unique joint entry marks the transfer of priestly authority from Moses (the founding officiant) to Aaron (the ongoing priest). When they come out and bless the people, the glory of YHWH appears to all the people — the theophany validates the entire ordination sequence and the inauguration of the sacrificial cult. The appearing of divine glory is the answer to the promise of Leviticus 9:4 and the culmination of the preparation that began at Exod 25.
24: Fire comes out from before YHWH and consumes the burnt offering and the fat pieces on the altar — divine fire (esh min YHWH) consuming the sacrifice is the sign of divine acceptance in key OT theophanic-sacrifice narratives: Gideon's offering (Judges 6:21), Elijah at Carmel (1 Kings 18:38), David at the threshing floor of Araunah (1 Chronicles 21:26), Solomon's temple dedication (2 Chronicles 7:1). The people's response — shouting and falling on their faces — combines the two poles of Israel's encounter with YHWH: joy and prostration, exultation and reverence. The same fire that appears in acceptance at Leviticus 9:24 appears in judgment at Leviticus 10:2 — divine glory manifests in both grace and holiness.
